elements.sent-2411-005: We compare heteroskedasticity-robust inference methods with a large-scale Monte Carlo study based on regressions from \nart\ reproduction packages of leading economic journals.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results confirm established wisdom and uncover new insights.
elements.sent-2411-007: Among well established methods HC2 standard errors with the degree of freedom specification proposed by Bell and McCaffrey (2002) perform best.
elements.sent-2411-008: To further improve the accuracy of t-tests, we propose a novel degree-of-freedom specification based on partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also show how HC2 to HC4 standard errors can be refined by more effectively addressing the \sharehione\% of cases where at least one observation exhibits a leverage of one.

elements.section-2411-002: Specifying Degrees of Freedom with Partial Leverages
elements.sent-2411-060: Various diagnostics have been proposed to ensure robust inference.
elements.sent-2411-061: One recommendation by MacKinnon et al. (2023b) is to report partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-062: Consider the linear regression model: \begin{equation}y=X\beta+\varepsilon,\label{eq:reg_long}\end{equation} where $y$ is the dependent variable, $X$ is the matrix of explanatory variables, $\beta$ is the vector of coefficients, and $\varepsilon$ represents the error term.
elements.sent-2411-063: Using the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) theorem, coefficient $\beta_{k}$ can be estimated with the simpler model: \begin{equation}\tilde{y}_{k}=\beta_{k}\tilde{x}_{k}+\tilde{\varepsilon}_{k},\label{eq:reg_fwl}\end{equation} where $\tilde{y _{k}$ and $\tilde{x _{k}$ are the residuals from regressing $y$ and $x_{k}$, respectively, on all other explanatory variables in $X$ except for $x_{k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Estimation of model (\ref{eq:reg_fwl ) yields the same OLS residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}$ and estimator $\hat{\beta _{k}$ as the original model (\ref{eq:reg_long ).\footnote{To enhance computational efficiency, the code for our Monte Carlo simulations and wild bootstrap specifications also extensively utilizes the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) representation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Although certain components, such as the hat matrix $H$ (see Section 3), cannot be derived from the FWL representation, these elements need to be computed only once per original regression.
elements.sent-2411-066: The FWL representations are particularly advantageous for computations that must be repeated for each Monte Carlo sample or each wild bootstrap sample.
elements.sent-2411-067: The partial leverage for observation $i$ with respect to explanatory variable $x_{k}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\tilde{h}_{k,i}=\frac{\tilde{x}_{k,i}^{2}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\tilde{x}_{k,j}^{2}},\end{equation} where $\tilde{x _{k,i}$ is the $i$-th element of $\tilde{x _{k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Furthermore, for $n\geq2$, we have $\tilde{h _{k,i <1$ if the original regression model includes a constant.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: To build intuition, consider an example where partial leverages are highly concentrated.
elements.sent-2411-071: Let $x_{k}$ be a dummy variable such that $x_{1,k =1$ and $x_{i,k =0$ for all $i=2,\ldots,n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Assume $x_{k}$ is uncorrelated with other explanatory variables.
elements.sent-2411-073: As the sample size $n$ grows, the partial leverage of the first observation $\tilde{h _{k,1}$ converges to 1.}
elements.sent-2411-074: More intuition about partial leverages can be gained by reformulating the OLS estimator as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{\beta}_{k}=\beta_{k}+\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sqrt{\tilde{h}_{k,i}}\,\tilde{\varepsilon}_{k,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\tilde{x}_{k,i}^{2}}}.\label{eq:beta_hat_h}\end{equation} We see that in the aforementioned example, where the partial leverage is concentrated on observation $i=1$, the variation in $\hat{\beta _{k}$ will be predominantly driven by the realization of $\tilde{\varepsilon _{1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: If high concentrations of partial leverage are not adequately accounted for in $t$-tests, the resulting rejection probabilities may deviate substantially from their nominal levels.
elements.sent-2411-076: A widely used concentration measure in competition policy is the Herfindahl-Hirschman index, defined as the sum of the squared market shares of all competitors in a market.
elements.sent-2411-077: Analogously, in our context, the concentration of partial leverages can be measured by the sum of squared partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-078: We denote the inverse of this measure as: \begin{equation}\tilde{n}_{k}=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\tilde{h}_{k,i}^{2}\right)^{-1}\end{equation} We refer to $\tilde{n _{k}$ as the partial-leverage-adjusted sample size.}
elements.sent-2411-080: If all observations have the same partial leverage then $\tilde{n _{k =n$ and in the limit case that the partial leverage becomes concentrated in a single observation $\tilde{n _{k}\rightarrow1$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-091: This section provides additional insights into the role of leverage, complementing the previous findings on partial leverage.
elements.sent-2411-092: We first present the mathematical formulations of robust standard errors HC0 to HC4.
elements.sent-2411-093: Consider the linear regression model: \begin{equation}y=X\beta+\varepsilon,\end{equation} where the error terms $\varepsilon_{i}$ are independently distributed with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{i ^{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The true variance-covariance matrix of $\hat{\beta}$ is given by: \begin{equation}Var(\hat{\beta})=(X^{\top}X)^{-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sigma_{i}^{2}x_{i}x_{i}^{\top}\right)(X^{\top}X)^{-1},\end{equation} where $x_{i}$ denotes the $i$-th row of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Heteroskedasticity-robust variance estimators of types HC0 to HC4 can all be expressed in the general form: \begin{equation}\hat{V}^{\tau}(\hat{\beta})=(X^{\top}X)^{-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_{i}^{\tau}\hat{\varepsilon}_{i}^{2}x_{i}x_{i}^{\top}\right)(X^{\top}X)^{-1},\end{equation} where $\hat{\varepsilon _{i}$ is the OLS residual for observation $i$, and $\alpha_{i ^{\tau}$ is an adjustment factor that depends on the specification $\tau$.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The corresponding standard errors are obtained as the square roots of the diagonal elements of $\hat{V ^{\tau (\hat{\beta )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The HC0 estimator, developed by Eicker (1967), Huber (1967), and White (1980), sets $\alpha_{i ^{HC0 =1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: While consistent, HC0 can exhibit severe bias in small samples and is rarely used in practice.

elements.sent-2411-211: The standard errors used in these regressions fall into three main categories: \sharehet\% heteroskedasticity-robust, \sharecluster\% cluster-robust, and \shareiid\% homoskedastic.

elements.sent-2411-213: For simplicity, we will often refer to heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors as robust standard errors.
elements.sent-2411-214: Panel B of Figure 1 illustrates the frequency of specific types of robust standard errors: \sharehcone\% of regression commands use Stata's default robust standard error, HC1.
elements.sent-2411-215: This finding suggests that common recommendations, see, for example, MacKinnon and White (1985), Chesher and Jewitt (1987), Chesher and Austin (1991), Long and Ervin (2000), or Cattaneo, Jansson, and Newey (2018), to adopt more robust alternatives in smaller samples, such as HC3, are rarely followed.
elements.sent-2411-216: From the reproduction packages containing an OLS regression with robust standard errors, we select the underlying regressions for the Monte Carlo study based on the following criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-217: The size of the reproduction package's ZIP file is below 10 MB.
elements.sent-2411-218: The regression command can be successfully run.
elements.sent-2411-219: The most common cause of a run error is the absence of confidential or proprietary data sets in the reproduction packages.
elements.sent-2411-220: A successful run of an automatic translation of the regression to R, based on the extracted information about the original regression.
elements.sent-2411-221: The second run must yield the same results as the original Stata run, except for small numerical discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-222: The entire reproduction, including the extraction and storage of regression related information and the 2nd reproduction run in R for all regressions, takes less than 15 minutes for the whole reproduction package.
elements.sent-2411-223: Reproduction runs that take longer are currently cancelled with a timeout.
elements.sent-2411-224: From each reproduction package with regressions meeting these criteria, at most four regressions are selected for the Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-225: For each regression, $t$-tests are performed on at most 25 regression coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-226: Each regression is estimated with a Least Squares Dummy Variable (LSDV) specification, even if the original Stata command uses absorbed fixed effects, to enable the computation of proper HC2, HC3, and HC4 standard errors.
elements.sent-2411-227: Additionally, heuristics are used to identify dummy variable sets that are not absorbed but look like fixed effects.
elements.sent-2411-228: For these dummy variables no t-tests are performed either.
elements.sent-2411-229: It is somewhat disappointing that we currently end up with only \nart\ reproduction packages containing at least one regression that satisfies all the criteria above.
elements.sent-2411-230: While some issues, such as missing data in a reproduction package, cannot be resolved, there remain additional points of failure in the process of reproducing the original Stata regression in R.
elements.sent-2411-231: As noted in the inital footnote, I develop a general toolbox named repbox in R to facilitate meta studies like the current one.
elements.sent-2411-232: Repbox does not only help to translate regression commands to R but also attempts to store the datasets used in the regressions in a manner that allows the data preparation steps to be automatically performed in R.
elements.sent-2411-233: This step is complex and provides another point of potential failure.
elements.sent-2411-234: Still, the \nart\ reproduction packages used as the basis for our Monte Carlo studies substantially exceed the scale of similar studies in economics that we are aware of.
elements.sent-2411-235: For example, the large-scale study by Young (2019) hand-collected reproducible regressions from 53 economic articles with experimental studies, while Young (2022) conducted large-scale Monte Carlo studies based on instrumental variables regressions from 30 economic articles.
elements.sent-2411-236: The number of test situations could be easily increased by selecting more than the current maximum of four regressions from each reproduction package.
elements.sent-2411-237: However, this approach risks skewing the results, as they may become more strongly influenced by a smaller number of reproduction packages that contain a large number of regressions.

elements.sent-2411-254: Compared to a feasible GLS specification based on a log-linear regression model, random forests offer greater flexibility and are well-suited to capture non-linear effects and interactions among explanatory variables in predicting heteroskedasticity.
elements.sent-2411-255: Since random forest predictions are always computed as averages of the dependent variable in the training dataset, they cannot produce negative standard errors.
elements.sent-2411-256: Whether the random forest predicts larger or smaller degrees of heteroskedasticity (measured by the variation in $\sigma_{i}$) depends on how the random forest is trained and how predictions are performed.
elements.sent-2411-257: Ordered from typically larger to smaller predicted heteroskedasticity, we consider five different candidates: in-sample prediction, out-of-bag prediction, out-of-bag prediction based on honest trees (see Athey et al., 2019), an equally weighted linear combination of out-of-bag prediction for honest trees and a homoskedastic model, and a purely homoskedastic model.

elements.sent-2411-259: One indicator of the approximation quality is the similarity between the sample distribution of the original OLS residuals and the distribution of the Monte Carlo OLS residuals.
elements.sent-2411-260: To evaluate candidate models, we particularly focus on the kurtosis of the OLS residuals: if the original error terms are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-261: More formally, let $\kappa^{m,r,c}$ denote the kurtosis of the OLS residuals in Monte Carlo sample $m$ for candidate model $c$ of original regression $r$.
elements.sent-2411-262: Let $\bar{\kappa ^{r,c}$ and $s_{\kappa ^{r,c}$ denote the corresponding mean and standard deviation of the kurtoses across all $M$ Monte Carlo samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: A basic distance measure for candidate model $c$ for original regression $r$ is the standardized distance \begin{equation}\delta^{c,r}=\frac{\left|\bar{\kappa}^{r,c}-\kappa^{r,o}\right|}{s_{\kappa}^{r,c}}\end{equation} where $\kappa^{r,o}$ is the kurtosis of the original OLS residuals.
elements.sent-2411-264: To some extent, this distance measure may favor data-generating processes that produce OLS samples with high variability in kurtosis.
elements.sent-2411-265: To counteract this effect, we also consider a modified distance measure: \begin{equation}\bar{\delta}^{c,r}=\frac{\left|\bar{\kappa}^{r,c}-\kappa^{r,o}\right|}{\bar{s}_{\kappa}^{r}}\end{equation} where $\bar{s _{\kappa ^{r}$ denotes the median of the kurtosis standard deviations across all candidate models for the original regression $r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: The final distance measure used is the average of these two measures: \begin{equation}d^{c,r}=0.5\delta^{c,r}+0.5\bar{\delta}^{c,r}\end{equation} The chosen DGP for original regression $r$ is the candidate model $c$ with the lowest distance $d^{c,r}$.

elements.section-2411-008: Appendix D: Motivating HC1-PL and HC2-PL by a Satterthwaite Approximation

elements.sent-2411-321: Analysing the Stata regression commands from \nstaticregart\ reproduction packages of leading economic journals, we find that, among the 40,571 regressions specifying heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors, \sharehcone\% adhere to Stata's default HC1 specification.
elements.sent-2411-322: We then compare several heteroskedasticity-robust inference methods with a large-scale Monte Carlo study based on regressions from \nart\ reproduction packages.
elements.sent-2411-323: Our results show that t-tests based on HC1 or HC2 with default degrees of freedom exhibit substantial over-rejection.
elements.sent-2411-324: Inference methods with customized degrees of freedom, as proposed by Bell and McCaffrey (2002), Hansen (2024), and a novel approach based on partial leverages, perform best.
elements.sent-2411-325: Additionally, we provide deeper insights into the role of leverages and partial leverages across different inference methods.
elements.sent-2411-326: A considerable body of literature has proposed and recommended different specifications for heteroskedasticity-robust inference.
elements.sent-2411-327: For instance, based on Monte Carlo evidence, Long and Ervin (2000) strongly recommend for sample sizes below 250 observations HC3 standard errors rather than Stata's robust default: HC1.\footnote{Similar recommendations to adopt more robust inference methods for smaller samples have been made e.g. by MacKinnon and White (1985), who introduced HC1, HC2 and HC3, Chesher and Jewitt (1987), Chesher and Austin (1991), and Cattaneo, Jansson, and Newey (2018).}
elements.sent-2411-328: Is this recommendation widely followed in empirical practice? No, the opposite holds true.

elements.sent-2411-348: While these results are insightful, several important questions remain.
elements.sent-2411-349: For instance, to what extent are HC1-based p-values excessively low, and to what extent are HC3-based p-values overly conservative? Beyond HC3 standard errors, alternative methods for improving robust inference have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-350: These include HC4 standard errors, introduced by Cribari-Neto (2004); HC2 standard errors with alternative calculations of degrees of freedom for the t-test, suggested by Bell and McCaffrey (2002) and Imbens and Kolesar (2016); Hansen's (2024) modified jackknife estimator, which also extend the degrees of freedom adjustments proposed by Bell and McCaffrey (2002); and wild bootstrap inference, as proposed e.g. by Wu (1986) and Roodman et al. (2019).
elements.sent-2411-351: Do some of these methods perform better than others across typical situations encountered in economic analyses? How heterogeneous is a method's performance across different situations? Do applied researchers encounter scenarios where these established approaches systematically fail, and novel methods offer meaningful improvements? To address these questions, we employ a large-scale Monte Carlo study.
elements.sent-2411-352: Monte Carlo simulations are a common tool in research on robust inference, but they typically examine only a small set of regression specifications, usually not based on real world data sets.\footnote{A notable exception is Young (2022), who conducts a large-scale Monte Carlo study based on instrumental variable regressions extracted from 30 reproduction packages of economic articles.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Already, in his previous article, Young (2019), hand-collected reproducible regressions from a large set of 53 reproduction packages, but did not yet base the Monte Carlo simulations on those regressions.
elements.sent-2411-354: We perform Monte Carlo studies based on a large set of \nreg\ OLS regressions originally conducted in \nart\ different reproduction packages of published economic articles.
elements.sent-2411-355: Ideally, our approach offers insights that are representative of the situations typically encountered by empirical researchers.
elements.sent-2411-356: Additionally, this broad scope enables us to investigate heterogeneity by examining how the performance of robust inference methods varies across the different original regression specifications.
elements.sent-2411-357: In a nutshell, for each original regression of the form \begin{equation}y^{o}=X\beta^{o}+\varepsilon^{o},\end{equation} we specify a custom data generating process \begin{equation}y=X\beta+\varepsilon\end{equation} with the same $n\times K$ matrix of explanatory variables $X$ as in the original regression.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Partial leverage adjusted degrees of freedom
elements.sent-2411-406: In our specifications HC1 to HC4 we follow Stata and other software packages and use $n-K$ degrees of freedom in the $t$-tests.
elements.sent-2411-407: Yet, it is evident from (\ref{eq:V_beta_k ) that if partial leverages are concentrated in a few observations, the standard errors are typically imprecisely estimated as they depend strongly on the residuals of those few observations.}
elements.sent-2411-408: Choosing simply $n-K$ degrees of freedom in the $t$-test does not account for such imprecisions arising from highly concentrated partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-409: The idea behind our newly proposed methods HC1-PL and HC2-PL is to base the degree of freedoms in the $t$-test on the concentration of partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-410: Similar to market shares, partial leverages are non-negative and add up to 1.
elements.sent-2411-411: Thus the concentration of partial leverages can be measured by the sum of squared partial leverages.
elements.sent-2411-412: If all observations have the same partial leverage then $\tilde{n _{k =n$, while $\tilde{n _{k =1$ in the limit case that the partial leverage becomes concentrated in a single observation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-413: For HC1-PL and HC2-PL we specify the degrees of freedom in the $t$-test as $\tilde{n _{k -1$ and use HC1 and HC2, respectively, as standard errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-414: Appendix~E provides a justification for this partial-leverage-based degrees of freedom by deriving it from a Satterthwaite approximation, similar to the approach of Bell and McCaffrey (2002).
elements.sent-2411-415: Figure~\ref{fig:rejection_vs_pl provides additional the empirical support for our proposal.}
elements.sent-2411-416: For the standard HC1-HC4 specifications, as well as for specification HC2-BM: all test situations with highly excessive rejection rates exhibit very small partial leverage adjusted sample sizes.
